schools.summary: Bethphage ('house of unripe figs') on Olivet's east slope; the Jericho road, arrival six days before Passover (John 12:1), and the dawn of Palm Sunday; the two unnamed disciples (perhaps Peter and John, Luke 22:8).
Calvin: Christ deliberately opens his reign on a borrowed ass to show a kingdom unlike earthly ones, intelligible only through Zechariah 9:9.
Henry: the poor provision bespeaks a kingdom not of this world; his one triumph was a ride toward death.

Remigius, Pseudo-Chrysostom, Jerome, Hilary: the bound ass = the synagogue under the Law, the unbroken colt = the Gentiles, loosed by the apostles; the two disciples = two Testaments/orders of preachers; Augustine harmonises the she-ass omitted by John.
Chrysostom: a lesson in contentment — a king who enters on an ass, unmatched among kings.

outliers.note: On the variation between Matthew (she-ass) and John: truth lies in the matter, not the exact words, given Matthew's Hebrew original and the Septuagint's freedom.

themes: ass and colt = Jew and Gentile (allegory)

schools.summary: The village (Bethany/Bethphage, or the tradition of Nob); only the colt named by Mark/Luke/John, never ridden (Mark 11:2); riding an ass as the peace-time dignity of kings (1 Kings 1:33; Judges 10:4).
Calvin: the borrowed ass serves a kingly self-display; Christ's foreknowledge of the beast and of the owner's consent proves his divinity; rejects the two-beast allegory.
Lightfoot: by Talmudic law unauthorised use would be robbery, so the willing surrender marks a miracle, not theft.

themes: Christ's foreknowledge and authority over hearts

schools.summary: Ellicott weighs three senses of 'the Lord' and prefers the disciples' special title; Robertson notes it is the lone Matthean ho kurios for Jesus and surveys its imperial/divine use; Barnes reads 'master' yet grants the owners knew Jesus's miracles.

debates.question: In what sense is 'the Lord' meant here?

sides.position: Christ as 'the Lord' in the disciples' special usage
Simply 'master,' a title of respect (though implying real authority)

schools.summary: The citation is composite (Isa 62:11 + Zech 9:9); Christ did not quote it himself (John 12:14ff.) — it is the Evangelist's comment; the disciples understood only later (John 12:16); the double purpose of prophecy.

outliers.note: Diverts to the third hour and Joel's prophecy of the Spirit poured out (Joel 2:28) — a Pentecost excursus, not a comment on the verse.

schools.summary: Daughter of Zion = the people/believing remnant; composite citation (Isa 62:11 + Zech 9:9); Hebrew parallelism, not two beasts; Ellicott on the peace-king of wider dominion (Zech 9:10) and the entry as concession-and-protest; Lightfoot on the paschal-lamb day and the rabbinic clouds/ass puzzle.
Calvin: the prophet marks the King by poverty, unlike the costly splendour of earthly kings.
Wesley glories in the lowly Prince of Peace (the war-horse reserved for Rev 19:11); Clarke: the triumph of humility over pride and malice.
Cyril of Jerusalem: the ass is the unique sign of this King, unlike all others in purple and chariots.

debates.question: Was the entry a concession to messianic hopes or a protest against them?

sides.position: Both — an apparent concession that was also a protest, for the kingdom is not of this world
A genuine, prophesied disclosure of a humble, peaceable King

schools.summary: Gill and Ellicott supply Mark/Luke's fuller scene — the colt tied at a public crossroads, the owners' question, and the procession beginning.
Calvin: the disciples' ready obedience against the fear of being thought thieves is a model of pressing through difficulty to obey, for God removes obstacles.

themes: obedient faith on a bare word

schools.summary: Harmony: Jesus rode the colt (Mark 11:7; Luke 19:35); 'them' = the garments, not two beasts; Clarke notes the variant 'upon him'; the garments as a saddle and as homage (Jehu, 2 Kings 9:13).

debates.question: Did Jesus ride one beast or both?

sides.position: The colt only; 'them' refers to the garments
Both, allegorically signifying Jews and Gentiles subjected to his sceptre

themes: garments as homage to a king
ass and colt = Jew and Gentile (allegory)

schools.summary: The crowd's composition (Jericho/Galilee, Bethany after Lazarus, Jerusalem with palms, John 12:13,17); garments and palms as royal homage (Jehu, 2 Kings 9:13); Barnes's palm symbolism of victory/peace; Lightfoot on the Tabernacles lulab (Lev 23:40).
Calvin: obscure people in earnest made fit heralds of Christ's kingdom; the branches a sudden movement of the Spirit, not a fixed rite.

outliers.note: Collects rabbinic legends of the ass — created at the world's twilight, ridden by Abraham, Moses, and the coming Messiah.

schools.summary: Hosanna = 'Save now' from Psalm 118:25–26 in the great Hallel; 'Son of David' proclaims the Messiah (and Jesus permits it); 'in the highest' echoes Luke 2:14; Ellicott collates the four Gospels' cries and Christ's tears (Luke 19:41).
Calvin: the cry is the Spirit-prescribed prayer for Christ's eternal kingdom (cf. 'Thy kingdom come'), known even to uneducated men.
Wesley: a divine impulse moved the crowd; Christ allowed the honour now as a testimony against those who would soon cry 'Crucify him.'
Cyril of Jerusalem: the children's witness establishes David's throne as the kingdom, not a seat of wood.
The city 'shaken' as by an earthquake (eseisthe) amid Passover pilgrims; 'Who is this?' shows Jerusalem's ignorance against the crowd's knowledge; Origen hears Psalm 24:8.
Calvin: the entry was public, the priests restrained by God-given fear, while the city's question betrays its careless indifference.

Pseudo-Chrysostom: the good Son hastens to his Father's house, the physician to the root of sickness (the priesthood); Raban: the entry five days before Passover answers the paschal lamb taken on the tenth (Exod 12:3).
The crowd, knowing Christ better than the rulers, names him 'the Prophet' foretold by Moses (Deut 18:18), a true confession yet short of King; the title with the article.
Wesley: 'of Nazareth' was a stumbling block, but earnest inquirers would learn he was of Bethlehem; Clarke marvels at the crowd's fickleness toward 'Crucify him.'
Jerome: they begin with the lesser (the Prophet like Moses) to come to the greater.

The traffic in the Court of the Gentiles — sacrificial animals and the half-shekel exchange (Exod 30:13); the Passover market; priests' rent and the changers' agio; the second cleansing harsher toward the dove-sellers (cf. John 2:16).
Chrysostom: two cleansings (John 2:14 the first), a heavier charge against Jews who persisted after being reproved twice.
Henry: the one act of regal, coercive authority, and the rule of reformation by Scripture to the primitive institution.
Calvin: Christ acts as King and High Priest, but private persons may only oppose the church's pollutions with the tongue, not the hand.

themes: traffic in the Court of the Gentiles

schools.summary: The composite citation (Isa 56:7 + Jer 7:11); the 'den of thieves' as the brigand-caves of Judaea (cf. 1 Sam 22:1) and exorbitant prices; Barnes's four reasons the traders submitted and the priests' murderous fear (Mark 11:18).
Calvin/Henry: the house of sacrifice was meant to be a house of prayer for all nations (Isa 56:7; cf. Isa 2:3) — Jeremiah's word strikes hypocrites who cloak iniquity in dissembled piety.
Wesley/Clarke: 'den of thieves' a proverb for a harbour of wicked men; a warning to negligent clergy of every age.

themes: house of prayer for all nations

schools.summary: The temple turned from market to 'hospital'; Ellicott resolves 2 Samuel 5:8 (no real exclusion of the blind/lame) and contrasts David's harshness with the Son of David's pity; the beggars at the gates (Acts 3:2); Isaiah 35:6.
Calvin: the miracles vouch Christ's claimed Messianic authority (the prophets' marks), God's endorsement of the people's praise — yet not for private men to imitate.

themes: healing as the temple's true use

schools.summary: The priests galled by both miracles and shouts; Clarke on the priesthood's habitual resistance to reform; Lightfoot on children taught from infancy to wave the bundles and sing Hosanna.
Calvin: their grievance is wounded authority — they resent the Messiah-title given to one they despise; God will raise witnesses (even stones) where the appointed messengers (Mal 2:7) fail.

Christ's 'Yea' accepts the homage; the citation of Psalm 8:2 from the LXX ('perfected praise' vs Hebrew 'ordained strength'); katertiso as in Matt 4:21; the Psalm applied to Christ (1 Cor 15:27; Eph 1:22; Heb 2:6); 'have ye never read?' a standing rebuke.
Calvin: God needs no orators but infants, whose very formation proclaims his power, as the dumb heavens do (Ps 19:1).

themes: out of the mouth of babes (Ps 8:2)

schools.summary: Pseudo-Chrysostom: bad men are checked by withdrawal, not reproof; Jerome on Christ's poverty, lodging with Lazarus; Origen: Bethany = the house of obedience, the Church, where Christ abides with the obedient and leaves the wicked.

From this day Christ lodged nightly at Bethany, not Jerusalem (Luke 21:37; John 11:18), avoiding snares and any suspicion of regal ambition; the home of Martha, Mary, and Lazarus a house of solace, their names suppressed in the Synoptics.
Henry: he left the priests as incorrigible — in prudence and in justice — for repining at Christ's praises drives him away.

outliers.note: Allegorises Bethany as the house of obedience (the Church): Christ abides with the obedient soul and forsakes the unbelieving.

schools.summary: The daybreak return to the city; real human hunger after a night likely spent outdoors in prayer, with food only from charity (Ellicott, Robertson, Clarke).

Calvin: the hunger was real, not feigned; as man he may not have known the tree; he overcame the flesh's hunger by zeal for the Father's glory (John 4:34), making the hunger an occasion for miracle and teaching.
Henry: he returned to a hostile city undeterred by foes or friends, consumed by zeal for God's house.

themes: faithful return to a hostile city
hunger as occasion for the sign

schools.summary: The roadside tree was common property (no owner wronged); the 'time of figs not yet' (Mark 11:13) met by the fig's multiple crops and the leaves promising early fruit (Hosea 9:10); 'curse' = devoting to barrenness (Heb 6:8); parachrema = immediately.
Calvin: the word condemns the tree to barrenness, the opposite of God's fertility-giving voice; the withering noticed next day (Mark).
Henry: the tree figures hypocrites (leaves without fruit) and the Jewish nation that owned Abraham but did not his works.

debates.question: Did Christ intend the fig tree as a symbol of the Jews / hypocrites?

sides.position: Yes — it embodies the parable of Luke 13:6 and the doom of fruitless Israel
It is a fit illustration, but there is no evidence Christ so intended it

schools.summary: The harmony with Mark 11:20 — the withered tree seen next morning, Peter the spokesman; the disciples marvel at the suddenness.
Henry: the withering had no visible cause but a 'secret blast,' figuring how gospel curses work silently yet effectually on rootless profession.
Clarke: the sudden judgment is a warning to be ready, answered by 'Lord, increase our faith.'

'Remove mountains' as the rabbinic proverb for overcoming difficulties (Lightfoot's 'rooters up of mountains'); the hyperbole excludes a literal sense (1 Cor 13:2; cf. Matt 17:20); 'doubt not' = not wavering; the fig tree as emblem of Israel.
Calvin: faith breaks into prayer and is a certainty relying on God's goodness without doubt (against Popish doubt, cf. Eph 3:12), yet prayer is bridled by the rule of God's word.

debates.question: Is 'remove this mountain' literal or proverbial?

sides.position: Proverbial — a vivid figure for overcoming great difficulty
Literally possible to the faith of miracles, though aimed at ministry's trials

outliers.note: Diverts to the ways of gaining eternal life (by faith, by preaching the gospel).

schools.summary: The promise turns on believing prayer (Robertson); its breadth (Gill) and the order of salvation (Clarke); Ellicott's implied condition of conformity to God's will (Matt 7:7) and the note that Christ's miracles came by power and prayer from the Father (John 11:42; Luke 8:46).

debates.question: Is the promise general to all believers or special to the apostles?

sides.position: Special to the apostles for working miracles; not to be claimed without limit
Extends to all that is for God's honour and the soul's good, within God's will

themes: miracles by power from the Father

schools.summary: The Sanhedrin's three elements confront him as he teaches; Ellicott on the rabbinic licence to teach (study 'at the feet' of a master, the key) which Jesus lacked; Henry on the ecclesiastical and civil courts joined to ensnare him.
Pseudo-Chrysostom: jealousy and the assumption that power comes only from men; Jerome: the two questions impute his works to the devil and deny the Son of God; the question met by a question.
Calvin: the demand for a divine calling is plausible (Ps 110:4; Heb 7:21) but malicious, since miracles had already attested Christ.
Origen: the priests probe the 'special degree' of power in Christ, who works all in one power from the Father; unworthy of the mystery, they get a counter-question.

themes: by what authority / who gave it

schools.summary: Christ was under no obligation to answer the obstinate; the counter-question takes the wise in their craftiness; 'one word' whose answer answers theirs; Ellicott on the logic of precedent — had they judged John?
Wesley: John's whole ministry was from God, and John testified that Jesus is the Christ — so the counter-question already settles theirs.
Henry: the prudence of the wise as serpents, giving a reason of our hope with caution lest truth be damaged.

themes: the baptism of John as test

schools.summary: 'Baptism of John' = his whole ministry; the imperfect dielogizonto paints their quandary; Ellicott shows the priests had long halted between two opinions about John (Matt 3:7; 11:18), unwilling to accept his witness to Jesus.

Calvin: John was Christ's herald (Mal 3:1; Luke 7:27), so to own John is to own Christ; the verse teaches that no doctrine or sacrament is received unless evidently from God — a rebuke to man-made sacraments.

themes: nothing in worship without divine warrant

schools.summary: Barnes: to call John's work imposture risked the people's violence, for all held John a prophet (Matt 14:5); Gill on the rejected alternative of a merely human ministry.
Henry: the people judged John more truly than the rulers, who, ruled by fear of man not God (Prov 29:25; Mal 2:9), weighed only their credit and safety.

themes: the second horn — fear of the people
the people's truer estimate of John
fear of man not of God

schools.summary: 'We cannot tell' was a deliberate lie ('we will not tell'), since owning John meant owning Christ; the confession of impotence is a virtual abdication, before which Christ need not plead.
Henry: those who imprison known truth in unrighteousness are justly denied further truth (Rom 1:18); Clarke: God hides from the wilfully blind what he reveals to babes.

A reproving parable that makes the hearers judge themselves; the father = God of all, the vineyard = righteousness, 'today' = this age; the two sons variously Gentiles/Jews or sinners/Pharisees (Jerome, Pseudo-Chrysostom).
Henry: like Nathan's parable to David (2 Sam 12:1), it judges offenders out of their own mouths; the vineyard is honourable work requiring present obedience.
Calvin: the parable strips the hypocrites' mask, that they may not boast of being God's ministers.
Origen: against those who promise much and do nothing, yet Israel is not finally shut out (Rom 11:25).

debates.question: Whom do the two sons represent?

sides.position: Gentiles (first) and Jews (second)
Penitent sinners/publicans and the self-righteous Pharisees/priests

schools.summary: The blunt 'I will not' of the openly irreligious; metamelomai (regretful change of purpose) carefully distinguished from metanoia (Robertson, Ellicott) — the first stage of repentance that here bore fruit (cf. 2 Cor 7:9; contrast Judas, Matt 27:3).
Gill: the refusal pictures the natural will, which has no inclination to good until grace turns it; the change shows grace toward the rebellious (1 Cor 6:11).
'I go, sir' is outward respect without performance, the Pharisees' surface religion; saying and doing are two things (Matt 23:3) — buds and blossoms are not fruit.
Calvin: the Hebrew idiom of ready service; prompt obedience is good, but this hypocrisy (promise unperformed) is less tolerable than the rebellion later subdued.
Wesley/Clarke: bare profession contradicted by action — what will it avail when God takes the soul?

The rulers' correct answer condemns themselves unawares; the first son preferred for deeds over words (Ezek 18:21); 'go before you' = lead the way (proagousin), with the door still open to follow; the publicans/harlots recall Matthew, Zacchaeus, the sinful woman.

outliers.note: Diverts to baptism as water-and-Spirit, the twofold cleansing of soul and body.

themes: publicans and harlots lead the way

schools.summary: 'Way of righteousness' as the Pharisees' own recognised righteousness (Matt 6:1) or the Jews' way of revelation; John's heaven-sent ministry proved by its fruit and success among publicans (Luke 3:12) and harlots (Luke 7:37); 'did not even repent.'
Calvin: faith is not bare assent but self-renouncing devotion to God; their refusal to follow even publicans and harlots aggravates their malice.
Wesley: the most notorious sinners were reformed, the professors not even convinced — 'how is this scripture fulfilled at this day!'

outliers.note: Diverts to the names Jesus and Christ as Saviour and Priest, typified by Joshua and Aaron.

themes: John in the way of righteousness

schools.summary: The parable rests on Isaiah 5:1-7 (vineyard = house of Israel); the wine-vat, hedge, and tower explained from Palestinian husbandry; Barnes cautions against over-allegorising details God did not interpret.

Jerome, Hilary, Pseudo-Chrysostom, Raban allegorise: householder = God, hedge = Law/angels, winepress = altar/word, tower = Temple/eminence of the Law, husbandmen = priests, the 'far country' = God leaving them freedom (not a change of place).
John Chrysostom: the parable shows God's long providence, Israel's murderous bent, the unity of the two Testaments, and the calling of the Gentiles with the casting out of the Jews.
Origen: unlike Isaiah, the Gospel blames the husbandmen, not the vineyard; the vineyard may be the kingdom/Scripture, its fruit a blameless life.
Calvin: God entrusts the vineyard's care to ministers and demands fruit; the priests, like swindlers, rob God of his due (cf. Isa 5:4; Jer 2:21).

themes: God's providence and the rulers' robbery

schools.summary: The servants = the prophets, the husbandmen = Israel's leaders; rent paid in kind by custom; the 'fruit' is the obedience God expected (Isa 5:4), Ellicott cautioning against over-precise parallels.
Wesley: the servants are the extraordinary messengers (prophets), the husbandmen the ordinary ministers of the Jews.

The three verbs picture the persecution of the prophets — flaying/scourging, killing (Isaiah sawn), stoning (Zechariah, 2 Chron 24:21); Lightfoot on the Sanhedrin's four deaths; the wider witness (Heb 11:37; Neh 9:26; Matt 23:37).
Calvin: God sent prophets 'in troops' against the priests' malice, exposing an obstinacy no remedy could cure.
'More than the first' read by Clarke as 'more honourable' (later prophets clearer about Christ); Ellicott on the fuller work of the later prophets to the Baptist; Gill suggests the Maccabean martyrs.
Henry: the riches of God's patience in keeping up a persecuted ministry, met by men who persist in sin, one sin making way for another.

themes: persistence of grace and of sin

schools.summary: The sending of the beloved Son (Mark 12:6; Luke 20:13) is the summit — a difference of kind, not degree (Heb 1:1-2); the mirror of God's love (John 3:16; 1 John 4:9).
Calvin: 'they will reverence' is an anthropomorphism; God knew the outcome, but the clause exposes how deplorable their rage against the Son was.

debates.question: Does 'they will reverence my son' imply ignorance or miscalculation in God?

sides.position: No — it is a parabolic anthropomorphism; God knew the outcome
It expresses what they ought to have done, exposing their guilt

themes: difference of kind from the prophets

schools.summary: The rulers largely knew the heir (John 11:48-49; 12:42) yet plotted his death to keep their power (the 'inheritance'); Christ knew their hearts; Barnes cautions the detail mainly carries the story (cf. Matt 27:1; Heb 1:2).

debates.question: Did the rulers kill the Son in ignorance or in knowing malice?

sides.position: In knowing malice — they recognised the heir and killed him from jealousy
Some acted in ignorance (cf. 1 Cor 2:8), though the leaders against conscience

schools.summary: The arrest in the garden, the delivery to the Romans (Acts 2:23; 7:52), and the crucifixion 'without the gate' (John 19:20; Heb 13:12; Luke 23:33) — the vineyard being Jerusalem; 'so they understood it' (v.45).
Clarke: to cast him out is to reject God's counsel, as every impenitent sinner does to keep his false peace.

themes: arrest and crucifixion of the Son
cast out of the vineyard = outside the gate

schools.summary: Gill and Barnes: the Lord comes in providence to reckon for the fruit, the prophets, and the murdered Son; the question is designed to make the rulers condemn themselves.
Henry: the Lord of the vineyard will certainly come and reckon; the appeal to the offenders themselves, like Nathan's, leaves them inexcusable.

The rulers' self-condemning verdict; the wordplay kakous kakos; the destruction fulfilled by Rome ~40 years later; the 'other husbandmen' = the Gentile church (Rom 11:11); an unconscious prophecy like Caiaphas's (John 11:49).
Henry: the doom fell on the Jews by the Romans and falls still on all who waste their church privileges.

debates.question: Who gave the answer, given Luke's 'God forbid'?

sides.position: The crowd was divided — some answered as Matthew, some as Luke (or Christ said it)
The priests answered with affected horror to disguise their conviction

schools.summary: Psalm 118:22-23, first of David then of Christ; the builders = the rulers, the rejected stone = Christ the corner-stone (Isa 28:16; Acts 4:11; Eph 2:20; 1 Pet 2:7); Ellicott extends it to the Gentile-church corner-stone (Eph 1:22).
Calvin: 'the Lord's doing' — Christ enthroned by God's power against men's will, remaining in his rank however assailed; Robertson calls it a withering indictment.

themes: God's power prevails over the builders

schools.summary: The kingdom/gospel taken from the Jews and given to the Gentiles (Acts 28:28); the 'fruits' are the returns of grace; Robertson calls it the death-knell of the nation's leadership.

Calvin: addressed now to the people too; a warning to all who presume on privilege (Rom 11:21), yet a comfort in the kingdom's perpetuity — God finds a dwelling elsewhere, and the gospel is meant to bear fruit.

outliers.note: Diverts to the treasure hidden in the field — the wisdom hidden in Christ and the Scriptures.

schools.summary: Two ruins: stumbling at the lowly Christ (Isa 8:14, recoverable) vs the stone falling in judgment (Dan 2:34-44, final, 'ground to powder' = winnowed as chaff); the Jewish stoning-scaffold imagery; the textual note (absent in some MSS, cf. Luke 20:18).
Calvin: Christ suffers no loss by rejection but breaks the obstinate, glorified in their ruin; we are bid yield gently to his dominion.
The rulers unmistakably perceived the parables were aimed at them (egnosan, ingressive) and were roused to revenge.

Pseudo-Chrysostom: the bad man grieves not at sin but at exposure, and rages at reproof; Raban sees it re-enacted in nominal churchmen; Origen distinguishes the murderous grasp from the Bride's loving hold (Song 3:4).
Calvin: threatenings inflame the ungodly, the word a hot iron to bad consciences; pray for a voluntary, tractable fear.

themes: they perceived he spoke of them
the word as a hot iron
two ways of laying hold of Jesus

schools.summary: The rulers longed to seize him but feared the crowd who held him a prophet (Luke 7:16; John 7:40); having been baffled before (John 7:44), they turn from open violence to stratagem.
Clarke: restraint only by fear is guilt in the heart; against it stands Christ's fearless testimony, and a defence of his entry against the charge of secular ambition (Isa 62:11; Zech 9:9).
